Metric: Software name? (string)
URL? (URL)
Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Response: Simbios, NIH National Center for Physics-Based Simulation of Biological Structures at Stanford University; primary developers Michael Sherman and Peter Eastman (Stanford Simbios Center); Scott Delp Neuromuscular Biomechanics Lab (Stanford)

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: High-performance open-source C++ multibody dynamics library for simulating articulated biomechanical and mechanical systems; provides accurate physics simulation of skeletons, robots, and vehicles; serves as the physics engine underlying OpenSim (biomedical simulation software)

Metric: Number of developers (all developers that have contributed at least one commit to the project) (use repo commit logs) (number)
How is the project funded? (unfunded, unclear, funded∗) where ∗ requires a string to say the source of funding
Initial release date? (date)
Last commit date? (date)
Status? (alive is defined as presence of commits in the last 18 months) (alive, dead, unclear)
License? (GNU GPL, BSD, MIT, terms of use, trial, none, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via a string
Platforms? (set of Windows, Linux, OS X, Android, other∗) ∗ given via string

Software Category? The concept category includes software that does not have an officially released version. Public software has a released version in the public domain. Private software has a released version available to authorized users only. (concept, public, private)
Development model? (open source, freeware, commercial, unclear)
Publications about the software? Refers to publications that have used or mentioned the software. (number or unknown)
Source code URL? (set of url, n/a, unclear)
Programming language(s)? (set of FORTRAN, Matlab, C, C++, Java, R, Ruby, Python, Cython, BASIC, Pascal, IDL, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via string
Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Simbody originates from NIH/Stanford work on biological simulation. It is the core physics engine for OpenSim, the industry-standard biomechanical simulation software used in clinical gait analysis and prosthetics research. Simbody handles compliant contact, constraint-based simulation, and articulated body inertia efficiently. Has 5,000+ commits. Primary use case is biomedical simulation but also applied to robotics.

Response: Conda install is the simplest path (single command). CMake from source is straightforward for a low-level C++ library. Documentation is clear and well-maintained. Primary audience is biomechanics researchers who may prefer OpenSim's GUI over direct Simbody access.

Response: Correctness is critically important for biomedical applications. Energy conservation and constraint satisfaction tests in the suite verify physics accuracy. Academic paper and NIH provenance provide strong correctness assurance.

Response: Simbody is remarkably stable given its role as the physics engine for OpenSim, which is used in clinical settings. Extensive testing driven by biomedical correctness requirements.

Response: Robust error handling appropriate for biomedical computing. Constraint violation errors include state information for debugging. Config errors in model files produce clear diagnostics.

Response: SimTK.org forum (simbody project page), GitHub Issues, documentation at simbody.github.io, OpenSim forum for users accessing Simbody through OpenSim

Response: Well-documented for its academic audience. Most users access Simbody indirectly through OpenSim rather than directly. The direct C++ API is well-understood by biomechanics and robotics researchers but requires domain expertise.

Response: Maintained by a small but dedicated team at Stanford with NIH support continuity. Simbody's central role in OpenSim ensures continued maintenance is essential. Slow release cadence but high stability.

Response: Clean C++ API with CMake integration. Used in OpenSim (clinical biomechanics), various robotics frameworks, and vehicle dynamics simulation. Apache-2.0 license supports commercial use.
